abstract: Learning good representations is essential for latent planning with world models. While pretrained visual encoders produce strong semantic visual features, they are not tailored to planning and contain information irrelevant -- or even detrimental -- to planning. Inspired by the perceptual straightening hypothesis in human visual processing, we introduce temporal straightening to improve representation learning for latent planning. Using a curvature regularizer that encourages locally straightened latent trajectories, we jointly learn an encoder and a predictor. We show that reducing curvature this way makes the Euclidean distance in latent space a better proxy for the geodesic distance and improves the conditioning of the planning objective. We demonstrate empirically that temporal straightening makes gradient-based planning more stable and yields significantly higher success rates across a suite of goal-reaching tasks.

citations.fullCitation: Hénaff, O. J., Goris, R. L. T., and Simoncelli, E. P. (2019). Perceptual straightening of natural videos. Nature Neuroscience.
citations.justification: This paper introduces the 'perceptual straightening hypothesis' in human visual processing, which serves as the core biological inspiration for the temporal straightening method proposed in the main paper. The authors explicitly state they are motivated by the idea that visual systems transform complex videos into straighter internal representations.
citations.title: Perceptual straightening of natural videos

citations.fullCitation: Zhou, G., Pan, H., LeCun, Y., and Pinto, L. (2025). Dino-wm: World models on pre-trained visual features enable zero-shot planning. ICML.
citations.justification: This work, DINO-WM, is the primary baseline and the closest related work discussed in the paper. The main paper's method is presented as a direct improvement over DINO-WM's approach of planning directly in a frozen DINOv2 feature space, showing that straightening the latent trajectories leads to significantly better planning outcomes.
citations.title: Dino-wm: World models on pre-trained visual features enable zero-shot planning

citations.fullCitation: Ha, D. and Schmidhuber, J. (2018). World models. arXiv preprint arXiv:1803.10122.
citations.justification: This is a foundational paper that popularized the concept of learning a compressed latent space model of the world for planning. The main paper's approach of learning an encoder and a predictive model to enable latent planning is a direct contribution to this established 'world model' paradigm.
citations.title: World models

citations.fullCitation: Oquab, M., Darcet, T., Moutakanni, T., Vo, H. V., Szafraniec, M., Khalidov, V., Fernandez, P., HAZIZA, D., Massa, F., El-Nouby, A., Assran, M., Ballas, N., Galuba, W., Howes, R., Huang, P.-Y., Li, S.-W., Misra, I., Rabbat, M., Sharma, V., Synnaeve, G., Xu, H., Jegou, H., Mairal, J., Labatut, P., Joulin, A., and Bojanowski, P. (2024). DINOv2: Learning robust visual features without supervision. TMLR.
citations.justification: This paper introduces the DINOv2 model, which provides the pretrained visual features used as a starting point by both the baseline and the proposed method. The main paper's key argument is that while DINOv2 features are powerful, they are not tailored for planning, thus motivating the development of the temporal straightening technique to improve them.
citations.title: DINOv2: Learning robust visual features without supervision

intermediateReport:
## Research Paper Analysis: Temporal Straightening for Latent Planning

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by a team of authors affiliated with multiple institutions:
*   Ying Wang, Oumayma Bounou, Gaoyue Zhou, Yann LeCun, and Mengye Ren are affiliated with New York University.
*   Randall Balestriero is affiliated with Brown University.
*   Tim G. J. Rudner is affiliated with the University of Toronto.
Yann LeCun and Mengye Ren are noted as equal advising authors.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

This research is situated within the field of reinforcement learning and control, specifically focusing on model-based approaches that leverage latent world models. Latent world models aim to address the challenges of planning in high-dimensional observation spaces (e.g., pixel data) by compressing these observations into lower-dimensional latent representations. Within this latent space, predictive dynamics models are learned, enabling agents to "imagine" future outcomes and optimize action sequences. This approach offers advantages in efficiency and generalization compared to planning directly in raw observation spaces.

Prior work in this area has explored various strategies for learning effective representations. Early methods often relied on reconstruction-based objectives (Watter et al., 2015; Zhang ets al., 2019; Ha and Schmidhuber, 2018), which reconstruct the original observations from their latent forms. However, these methods can overemphasize low-level visual details that are not always relevant for planning. More recent approaches, such as DINO-WM (Zhou et al., 2025), have explored using powerful pre-trained visual encoders (like DINOv2) to extract semantic features, and then learning dynamics in this frozen feature space. While effective, these pre-trained features are not explicitly optimized for the specific task of planning within a learned dynamics model.

The paper also connects to the Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) paradigm (LeCun, 2022; Bardes et al., 2024), which focuses on learning representations by predicting future or masked parts of input without explicit reconstruction. This paradigm aims to capture predictable structure while discarding unpredictable, task-irrelevant details. Temporal Straightening aligns with the JEPA family by focusing on learning representations optimized for prediction and subsequent planning.

Furthermore, the work draws inspiration from the "perceptual straightening hypothesis" in human vision (Hénaff et al., 2019), which suggests that biological visual systems internally straighten complex visual trajectories. While some prior works have examined implicit straightening in visual encoders (Harrington et al., 2023) or used it as an objective for robust video models (Niu et al., 2024), this paper specifically investigates and quantifies the direct impact of explicitly promoting straightened latent trajectories on the performance of gradient-based planning.

The broader landscape includes alternative representation learning methods like Temporal Contrastive Learning (Sermanet et al., 2018), which uses contrastive losses to encourage temporal coherence in embeddings. However, these methods often require careful selection of positive and negative samples and can potentially misrepresent geodesic distances. The current work presents a regularization-based approach that does not require negative samples or expert trajectories.

In summary, this research addresses a recognized limitation in latent world models—the difficulty of optimizing in highly curved latent spaces—by introducing a novel geometric regularization technique informed by a neuroscientific hypothesis, and positions itself as a refinement within the JEPA paradigm to improve representations specifically for efficient gradient-based planning.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to improve representation learning for latent planning in model-based reinforcement learning through the introduction of a novel technique termed "temporal straightening."

The motivation for this work stems from several challenges encountered in current latent planning methodologies:

*   **Difficulty of Optimization in Latent Space:** While latent world models offer abstraction and efficiency, planning within their learned latent spaces often remains challenging. The induced planning objectives are typically highly non-convex, which can impede the convergence and stability of gradient-based optimizers. This often necessitates the use of computationally expensive search-based methods, such as the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) or Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI), which increase computational burden and latency.
*   **Misleading Latent Distance Metrics:** A common practice in latent planning is to define a cost function based on the Euclidean distance between a predicted terminal latent state and a target goal latent state. However, if the latent embedding space is highly curved, the straight-line Euclidean distance in this space can inaccurately represent the actual "geodesic" distance—the shortest feasible path distance—between states. This misalignment can lead planners astray, resulting in suboptimal or failed plans. As depicted in the paper, latent trajectories encoded by existing visual encoders are often highly curved, making Euclidean distance a poor proxy for progress towards a goal.
*   **Limitations of Current Representation Learning Strategies:**
    *   **Pre-trained Visual Encoders:** While powerful pre-trained visual encoders (e.g., DINOv2) provide semantically rich features, they are not inherently optimized for the temporal dynamics or planning objectives of a specific environment. They may retain information irrelevant to planning, or even detrimental, by introducing complex geometric distortions in the latent space.
    *   **Reconstruction-Based Objectives:** Traditional reconstruction objectives, which aim to reconstruct the original high-dimensional observations from latents, can overly emphasize low-level visual details that are not essential for higher-level planning.
    *   **Lack of Geometric Regularization:** Existing methods often lack explicit mechanisms to shape the geometry of the latent space to facilitate planning.

To address these issues, the authors propose that a "good" representation for latent planning should possess specific characteristics:
1.  **Sufficiency for Dynamics Prediction:** The representation must contain enough information to accurately predict future states and actions, but without retaining task-irrelevant details.
2.  **Proper Regularization for Distance Alignment:** The latent space should be regularized such that Euclidean distances in this space reliably reflect the geodesic distances relevant to feasible transitions within the environment.
3.  **Amenability to Gradient-Based Optimization:** The representation should yield a planning objective that is better conditioned and less non-convex, thereby enabling more reliable and efficient gradient-based optimization.

Inspired by the perceptual straightening hypothesis in human vision, the authors hypothesize that explicitly encouraging latent trajectories to be "straighter" will naturally lead to representations possessing these desired properties. This motivation aims to unlock the full potential of differentiable latent world models for efficient, gradient-based planning.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology centers on jointly training a world model with a novel geometric regularization term called "temporal straightening."

**World Model Architecture:**
The world model comprises three main components:
*   **Sensory Encoder (E_s_ϕ):** This component maps raw high-dimensional observations (o_t) to a compact latent representation (z_t ∈ R^d). Two primary setups are explored:
    1.  A pre-trained visual backbone (DINOv2) with a trainable lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) projector (P_ϕ) applied on top. The projector can reduce spatial resolution and/or channel dimension, generating features z_v_t ∈ R^(m_v × d_v).
    2.  A ResNet architecture trained from scratch to produce the latent features z_v_t ∈ R^(m_v × d_v) directly.
*   **Action Encoder (E_a_ψ):** This maps raw actions (a_t ∈ R^(n_a)) to latent action embeddings (∈ R^(d_a)).
*   **Predictor (f_θ):** A Vision Transformer (ViT) is utilized as the dynamics predictor. It takes a history of K past latent states and action embeddings to predict the next latent state (ˆz_t). If proprioceptive states are available, they are also encoded and concatenated with visual features. A temporal causal attention mask is applied to ensure autoregressive prediction.

**Temporal Straightening (Geometric Regularization):**
The core of the proposed method is a regularization term designed to encourage local straightness in latent trajectories.
*   **Latent Velocities:** For three consecutive latent representations (z_t, z_{t+1}, z_{t+2}), approximate latent velocity vectors are defined as v_t = z_{t+1} - z_t and v_{t+1} = z_{t+2} - z_{t+1}.
*   **Curvature Metric:** Curvature is penalized by minimizing the angle between these consecutive velocity vectors. This is achieved by maximizing their cosine similarity (C = (v_t ⋅ v_{t+1}) / (||v_t||_2 ⋅ ||v_{t+1}||_2)).
*   **Straightening Objective (L_curv):** The loss function for straightening is defined as L_curv = 1 - C, where minimizing this term encourages the cosine similarity to approach 1 (i.e., making the vectors collinear).
*   **Application to Spatial Features:** For visual features that retain spatial structure (m_v > 1 patches), various strategies for computing cosine similarity were ablated: per-patch averaging, mean-pooling, flattening, and using a learnable pooling head (h_ϕ). The learnable pooling head, which aggregates spatial features into a single global vector before computing cosine similarity, was found to perform best. This approach applies straightening to the global trajectory representation while allowing local patch variations.

**Training Objective:**
The world model components (E_s_ϕ, E_a_ψ, f_θ) are trained jointly to minimize a total loss:
*   **Prediction Objective (L_pred):** A Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss between the predicted next latent state (ˆz_{t+1}) and the actual next latent state (z_{t+1}). A stop-gradient (sg) operation is applied to the target latent (sg(z_{t+1})) to prevent representational collapse.
*   **Overall Objective (L_total):** L_total = L_pred + λL_curv, where λ is a hyperparameter controlling the regularization strength of temporal straightening.

**Theoretical Analysis:**
The paper provides a theoretical analysis to support the efficacy of straightening.
*   **Linear Dynamics Assumption:** For tractability, a linear latent dynamical system (z_{t+1} = Az_t + Ba_t) is considered.
*   **ε-straight Transition:** A transition is defined as ε-straight if the spectral norm of (A - I) is small (||A - I||_2 ≤ ε), implying that the state evolution is close to an identity mapping perturbed by actions.
*   **Hessian Conditioning:** Theorem 4.4 demonstrates that for ε-straight transitions, the effective condition number of the planning Hessian (∇^2_a L(a)) is bounded. A smaller ε leads to a better-conditioned Hessian, whose growth with the planning horizon is slower. This directly translates to faster convergence rates for gradient-based optimizers.
*   **Cosine Similarity as Proxy:** Proposition C.9 shows that maximizing cosine similarity (the practical regularization term) implies that (A - I) is small along the visited velocity directions, connecting the empirical objective to the theoretical condition for improved Hessian conditioning.

**Planning with Straightened Dynamics:**
*   **Gradient Descent (GD) Planner:** The trained world model is used for planning by optimizing an action sequence (a = (a_0, ..., a_{K-1})) over a horizon K. The objective is to minimize the MSE between the predicted terminal latent state and a target goal latent state (L(a) = ||z_K - z_g||_2^2). Gradient descent is applied to update the action sequence.
*   **Model Predictive Control (MPC):** In a closed-loop setting, the agent repeatedly plans an action sequence, executes only the first action(s), observes the new state, and replans. A weighted objective function is used in MPC to encourage the predicted trajectory to approach the target.

**Experimental Setup:**
Experiments are conducted on four 2D goal-reaching environments: Wall, PointMaze (UMaze and Medium-Maze), and PushT. A novel Teleported-PointMaze environment is also introduced to specifically test the model's ability to learn dynamic-aware representations beyond visual similarity. Baselines include DINO-WM with frozen DINOv2 features. Performance is evaluated using open-loop and MPC success rates.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The experiments demonstrate that temporal straightening significantly improves the properties of the learned latent space and enhances planning performance across various tasks and architectures.

**5.1. Embedding Space Characteristics:**
*   **Reduced Curvature:** The straightening regularization effectively reduces the curvature of latent trajectories. Quantitative analysis (Figure 5) shows higher average cosine similarities between consecutive latent velocity vectors, indicating straighter paths, particularly when explicit regularization is applied. Visualizations using PCA (Figures 2, 14-17) also depict smoother, less curved trajectories in the straightened latent space compared to baseline DINOv2 embeddings. Implicit straightening is observed even without explicit regularization, attributed to the predictive JEPA objective.
*   **Faithful Distance Metric:** Temporal straightening improves the alignment between Euclidean distances in the latent space and the true geodesic distances in the environment. Distance heatmaps (Figures 6, 12, 13, 23) show that straightened representations produce distance landscapes that closely approximate the minimum number of steps required to reach a target, similar to ground-truth A-star distances. This suggests that the latent space becomes a more reliable metric space for planning.
*   **Sufficient Information Preservation:** The representations, even when aggressively compressed (e.g., DINOv2 features with channel dimensions reduced from 384 to 8), retain sufficient information to allow for high-fidelity reconstruction of observations via a separate decoder. This indicates that the critical planning-relevant details are preserved while irrelevant information may be discarded.
*   **Improved Loss Landscape:** Empirically, straightening is observed to yield an action-space loss landscape with reduced non-convexity (Figure 4). This supports the theoretical analysis that straightening improves the conditioning of the planning Hessian, making gradient-based optimization more tractable.

**5.2. Planning Performance:**
*   **Significant Success Rate Gains:** Temporal straightening consistently leads to substantial improvements in goal-reaching success rates for both open-loop and Model Predictive Control (MPC) planning using a simple gradient descent (GD) planner (Table 1).
    *   Open-loop success rates improved by 20–60% and MPC success rates by 20–30% compared to baselines using equivalent GD planners.
    *   For example, in PointMaze-UMaze with a DINOv2 projector, open-loop success increased from 44.00% to 94.00%. When training a ResNet from scratch for UMaze, open-loop success increased from 14.67% to 64.67%.
*   **Accelerated MPC Performance:** The straightened models achieve high success rates rapidly with MPC, demonstrating efficient trajectory discovery. In environments like Wall and PointMaze-UMaze, 100% success is reached within a few MPC steps (Figure 8).
*   **Reduced Gap with Search-Based Methods:** While search-based methods like CEM generally outperform GD, temporal straightening substantially reduces this gap (Table 3). Straightened models using GD can achieve performance competitive with or sometimes superior to unstraightened models using CEM, highlighting the efficiency gains of improved latent space geometry.
*   **Long-Horizon Planning:** For longer planning horizons (50 steps), straightened models consistently outperform baselines, although overall success rates naturally decrease due to compounding prediction errors (Table 2). This indicates improved stability for extended rollouts.

**5.3. Architectural and Hyperparameter Insights:**
*   **Impact of Encoder Choice:** Training a ResNet from scratch with straightening generally yielded lower curvature and high success rates, suggesting greater flexibility in adapting the representation geometry to dynamics compared to a fixed DINOv2 backbone with a projector.
*   **Spatial Structure and Feature Dimensions:** Retaining spatial structure in latent features (e.g., DINOv2 patches) is more effective than collapsing them into a single global vector. Moderate channel dimensions (e.g., 8 channels for projected DINOv2 features) are optimal; very low dimensions are insufficient, while excessively high dimensions (e.g., 128 channels) can hinder planning performance (Figure 10).
*   **Learnable Pooling Head:** For spatial features, applying straightening to features aggregated by a learnable pooling head (h_ϕ) performed best, as it encourages global straightness without overly constraining local patch-level variations (Figure 11).

**5.4. Teleported-PointMaze Validation:**
In the Teleported-PointMaze environment, which introduces non-visual dynamics (teleportation), the straightened model successfully leverages the teleportation mechanic to reach targets (Figures 23, 24). This demonstrates that the method learns dynamics-aware representations rather than merely relying on visual similarity, which would be misleading in this modified environment.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

This research presents temporal straightening as an effective technique for improving representation learning in latent world models, with several significant implications:

*   **Enabling Efficient Gradient-Based Planning:** The primary impact is its ability to make gradient-based planning more stable and effective. By regularizing the latent space to reduce curvature and improve the conditioning of the planning objective, the method reduces the reliance on computationally intensive search-based planning algorithms (like CEM). This could significantly decrease the computational cost and latency associated with planning, making model-based reinforcement learning more practical for real-time applications and resource-constrained environments, such as robotics.
*   **Improved Latent Space Geometry for Control:** The work underscores the importance of the geometric properties of the latent space for control tasks. It demonstrates that explicitly shaping this geometry through temporal straightening can create a space where Euclidean distances more faithfully represent true geodesic distances, thus providing more reliable signals for planners. This finding suggests a new direction for representation learning in control, moving beyond mere predictive accuracy to optimize for the downstream planning process itself.
*   **Bio-Inspired AI:** Drawing inspiration from the "perceptual straightening hypothesis" in human vision offers a compelling example of how insights from neuroscience can inform the design of more effective artificial intelligence systems. This connection opens avenues for further interdisciplinary research between cognitive science and machine learning, particularly in areas of perception, action, and internal model learning.
*   **Robustness to Complex Dynamics:** The successful application in the Teleported-PointMaze environment highlights the method's potential to learn dynamics-aware representations even when visual similarity is decoupled from temporal distance. This suggests improved robustness in environments with non-intuitive or complex underlying dynamics.
*   **Foundation for Future Research:** The theoretical analysis provides a foundation for understanding why straightening is beneficial, particularly in linear systems. Extending this analysis to more complex, non-linear dynamics, potentially by controlling higher-order terms or products of state-dependent Jacobians, represents a promising direction for future theoretical work. Experimentally, applying this method to more complex, high-dimensional, and real-world robotic tasks could further validate its scalability and generalizability.
*   **Practical Applicability:** For researchers and practitioners, temporal straightening offers a straightforward and implementable regularization term that can be integrated into existing latent world model training pipelines to enhance performance without requiring complex architectural changes or additional negative sampling strategies.
overview:
## Overview

This paper introduces temporal straightening, a geometric regularization technique that improves representation learning for latent world models in model-based reinforcement learning. The method addresses a fundamental challenge in latent planning: how to learn representations that not only capture the essential dynamics of an environment but also enable efficient gradient-based optimization for planning tasks.

The core insight stems from neuroscience, specifically the "perceptual straightening hypothesis" which suggests that biological visual systems internally straighten complex visual trajectories. The authors apply this principle to artificial systems by regularizing latent trajectories to be geometrically straighter, thereby creating representations that are more amenable to planning algorithms.

## Problem Setting and Motivation

Latent world models aim to address the computational challenges of planning in high-dimensional observation spaces by learning compressed representations of the environment. While these models offer efficiency benefits over planning directly in raw pixel space, they introduce a new challenge: the learned latent spaces are often highly curved, making gradient-based optimization difficult and unreliable.

Current approaches face several limitations. Pre-trained visual encoders like DINOv2, while semantically rich, are not optimized for the specific requirements of planning tasks. Reconstruction-based methods can overemphasize low-level visual details irrelevant to planning. Most critically, when latent trajectories are curved, the Euclidean distance between states in latent space poorly approximates the true geodesic distance - the shortest feasible path between states in the actual environment.

This misalignment between latent geometry and true state relationships leads to suboptimal planning. Gradient-based optimizers struggle with the highly non-convex planning objectives that result from curved latent spaces, often requiring computationally expensive search-based methods like the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) or Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI).

## Methodology

The temporal straightening approach centers on a joint training objective that combines standard predictive modeling with a geometric regularization term designed to encourage straighter latent trajectories.

**World Model Architecture**

The world model consists of three primary components:
- A sensory encoder that maps high-dimensional observations to compact latent representations
- An action encoder that embeds actions into the latent space  
- A Vision Transformer predictor that learns the latent dynamics

Two encoder setups are explored: using a pre-trained DINOv2 backbone with a trainable projector, and training a ResNet architecture from scratch.

**Temporal Straightening Regularization**

The key innovation is a regularization term that penalizes curvature in latent trajectories. For three consecutive latent states $z_t$, $z_{t+1}$, and $z_{t+2}$, the method defines velocity vectors:

$$
v_t = z_{t+1} - z_t, \quad v_{t+1} = z_{t+2} - z_{t+1}
$$

The straightening objective maximizes the cosine similarity between consecutive velocity vectors:

$$
C = \frac{v_t \cdot v_{t+1}}{||v_t||_2 \cdot ||v_{t+1}||_2}
$$

The regularization loss is then:

$$
L_{curv} = 1 - C
$$

This encourages consecutive velocity vectors to be collinear, effectively straightening the trajectory. For spatial features with multiple patches, a learnable pooling head aggregates features before computing the cosine similarity.

The total training objective combines prediction accuracy with straightening:

$$
L_{total} = L_{pred} + \lambda L_{curv}
$$

where $L_{pred}$ is the mean squared error between predicted and actual next states, and $\lambda$ controls the regularization strength.

**Theoretical Foundation**

The paper provides theoretical justification for why straightening improves planning. Under a linear dynamics assumption where $z_{t+1} = Az_t + Ba_t$, the authors define an "ε-straight" transition as one where $||A - I||_2 \leq \varepsilon$. They prove that for such transitions, the condition number of the planning Hessian is bounded, leading to faster convergence for gradient-based optimizers. The straightening regularization effectively encourages this condition by making the latent dynamics closer to identity mappings perturbed by actions.

## Experimental Validation

The method is evaluated on four 2D goal-reaching environments: Wall, PointMaze variants (UMaze and Medium-Maze), PushT, and a custom Teleported-PointMaze environment designed to test dynamics-aware representation learning.

**Embedding Space Analysis**

Quantitative analysis confirms that temporal straightening successfully reduces trajectory curvature. The regularized models show higher average cosine similarities between consecutive velocity vectors compared to baselines. Principal Component Analysis visualizations reveal smoother, less curved trajectories in the straightened latent space.

Importantly, the method improves the alignment between Euclidean distances in latent space and true geodesic distances in the environment. Distance heatmaps show that straightened representations produce landscapes closely approximating the minimum steps required to reach targets, similar to ground-truth A-star distances.

**Planning Performance**

The results demonstrate substantial improvements in planning success rates:

- Open-loop planning success rates improved by 20-60% across tasks
- Model Predictive Control (MPC) success rates increased by 20-30%
- For PointMaze-UMaze with DINOv2 projector, open-loop success increased from 44% to 94%
- When training ResNet from scratch, success rates jumped from 15% to 65%

The straightened models achieve high performance rapidly with MPC, reaching 100% success within a few steps in simpler environments. Crucially, the method substantially reduces the performance gap between gradient-based and search-based planning methods, sometimes achieving comparable results with much lower computational cost.

**Architecture Insights**

Training encoders from scratch with straightening generally yields better results than using fixed pre-trained backbones, suggesting the importance of jointly optimizing representation and dynamics. Moderate feature dimensions (around 8 channels) prove optimal - too few channels are insufficient while too many can hinder planning performance. For spatial features, applying straightening to globally aggregated representations while preserving local patch-level flexibility works best.

## Validation on Complex Dynamics

The Teleported-PointMaze environment provides a critical test case where visual similarity is decoupled from temporal dynamics. In this environment, the agent can teleport between visually similar locations, making visual-similarity-based planning misleading. The straightened model successfully learns to leverage the teleportation mechanic, demonstrating that it captures dynamics-aware representations rather than merely visual patterns.

## Significance and Impact

This work makes several important contributions to model-based reinforcement learning and representation learning more broadly. By demonstrating that geometric properties of latent spaces significantly impact planning performance, it opens new research directions focused on optimizing representations specifically for downstream control tasks rather than just predictive accuracy.

The bio-inspired approach provides a compelling example of translating neuroscientific insights into practical AI improvements. The method's ability to enable efficient gradient-based planning could substantially reduce computational requirements for model-based RL, making it more practical for real-time applications like robotics.

The theoretical analysis connecting trajectory straightness to planning optimization provides a foundation for future work on geometric properties of learned representations. The consistent improvements across different architectures and environments suggest the approach's generalizability, while the straightforward implementation makes it readily adoptable by practitioners.

Perhaps most significantly, this work challenges the common assumption that any sufficiently expressive representation will work for planning, instead demonstrating that the geometric structure of the latent space is crucial for optimization success. This insight could influence how the field approaches representation learning for control tasks more broadly.

keyInsights: Explicitly promoting "straighter" latent trajectories (by penalizing curvature) significantly improves the conditioning of the planning objective's Hessian, enabling more efficient gradient-based optimization.
Regularizing the latent space geometry creates a more faithful distance metric where Euclidean distances between latent states accurately reflect the true geodesic distances in the environment.
The "perceptual straightening hypothesis" from human vision offers a valuable inspiration for designing latent spaces that facilitate efficient planning in artificial intelligence systems.
originalProblem: Planning in learned latent spaces often involves highly non-convex objectives, hindering efficient gradient-based optimization and forcing reliance on computationally expensive search methods.
Euclidean distances in existing curved latent spaces can inaccurately represent true geodesic distances, leading to suboptimal action sequences from planners.

Current representation learning methods, such as reconstruction or frozen pre-trained features, may not effectively optimize the latent space geometry for efficient temporal dynamics and gradient-based planning.
results: Temporal straightening consistently improved goal-reaching success rates by 20-60% for open-loop planning and 20-30% for Model Predictive Control with gradient-based optimizers across various 2D environments.

The method substantially reduced the performance gap between gradient descent and computationally more intensive search-based planning algorithms like CEM, sometimes outperforming unstraightened CEM baselines.

Learned representations exhibited reduced curvature, and their latent Euclidean distances closely approximated ground-truth environmental geodesic distances, even in environments with non-visual dynamics like Teleported-PointMaze.
solution: A novel "temporal straightening" geometric regularization term is proposed, which explicitly minimizes the angle between consecutive latent velocity vectors.
A world model, comprising a sensory encoder (e.g., DINOv2 projector or ResNet) and a Vision Transformer predictor, is trained jointly with a prediction loss and this straightening loss.

For visual features retaining spatial structure, a learnable pooling head aggregates patch-level representations into a global vector before applying the straightening penalty, balancing local detail with global straightness.
summary.summary: Researchers introduced "temporal straightening," a geometric regularization technique that encourages straighter trajectories in latent space, improving representations for model-based reinforcement learning. This method, inspired by the perceptual straightening hypothesis, significantly enhances gradient-based planning performance by creating a latent space where Euclidean distances more accurately reflect true environmental distances, leading to substantial gains in goal-reaching success rates.

title: Temporal Straightening for Latent Planning
